segments.title: Why the T.2 Dart suited carrier decks
segments.summary: The Blackburn T.2 Dart paired gentle low-speed handling with deck-landing features that made a large single-seat aircraft workable aboard carriers.
segments.body: Although unusually large for a single-seat aircraft, the Dart is presented as a pilot-friendly machine with subtle, manageable handling. Its wing loading was only nine pounds per square foot, helping it remain controllable at low speed. A 38-knot stalling speed made landing on an aircraft-carrier deck comparatively forgiving. From 1928, each aircraft received Handley Page slots when it came up for reconditioning, further improving the type's low-speed deck-landing qualities.

evidence.note: The transcript describes the Dart's handling, low wing loading, 38-knot stalling speed, carrier-deck landing qualities, and Handley Page slots.

segments.title: A dependable platform for torpedo technique
segments.summary: The Dart's ten years of rugged service gave the Fleet Air Arm a dependable aircraft for refining torpedo-dropping technique.
segments.body: The Dart was not a high-performance type, but the video stresses the value of its rugged dependability over ten years in service. That long service life gave the Fleet Air Arm a stable aircraft on which pilots could repeatedly practise aerial-torpedo work. The type therefore helped the service develop and perfect torpedo-dropping techniques despite its modest performance. The passage shows why reliability and continuity can matter more to tactical development than speed or headline performance.

evidence.note: The transcript contrasts the Dart's modest performance with ten years of dependable service and its role in developing Fleet Air Arm torpedo-dropping techniques.

segments.title: Practising the low-level torpedo approach
segments.summary: At Gosport, a Dart-equipped torpedo training flight taught naval pilots the close-to-the-water approach needed for an attack run.
segments.body: D Torpedo Training Flight at Gosport taught naval pilots to flatten out only 15 feet above the water. From that height, the pilot aimed the whole aircraft at the target to establish the torpedo attack run. The description makes the demanding low-level geometry of early aerial-torpedo delivery concrete. The training-flight aircraft were the exception visible ashore, because most Darts normally served at sea with aircraft carriers.

evidence.note: The transcript identifies D Torpedo Training Flight at Gosport, its 15-foot-low torpedo-approach instruction, and the Dart's normal carrier service.

segments.title: Making the Dart visible at air displays
segments.summary: A handful of early-1920s displays brought the carrier-borne Dart before the public, culminating in a five-aircraft mock torpedo attack from HMS Furious.
segments.body: Dart N9549, then coded 1, appeared at the Hendon RAF display on 30 June 1923. Another aircraft carried a dummy torpedo at a Croydon display for Dominion premiers that November, while newly built N9689 was exhibited at Prague's Palace of Industry in 1924. At Hendon on 20 June 1924, five Darts from HMS Furious staged a mock torpedo attack on the dummy enemy ship Slavic before thousands of spectators. N9549 led the formation again, now coded 4 rather than 1. These events made carrier-borne torpedo aviation visible to the public even though most Darts normally operated at sea.

evidence.note: The transcript lists Dart appearances at Hendon, Croydon, and Prague, then describes five HMS Furious aircraft making a mock torpedo attack on the dummy ship Slavic at Hendon in 1924.
